race.name: Tour of Anchorage
race.tagline: Fifty kilometers from mountains to sea — a point-to-point urban odyssey through Alaska's wild heart.

vitals.aid_stations: 5-6 aid stations with warm drinks, food, and emergency gear

climate.description: March in Anchorage brings lengthening daylight but persistent cold. Temperatures range from -15°C to 0°C. The Chugach Mountains create variable conditions — sheltered valleys versus exposed coastal trail. Chinook winds can cause rapid temperature swings.
challenges: Subarctic wind chill on exposed coastal trail sections
Highly variable snow conditions — packed trail to fresh powder
Moose encounters on urban wilderness trail sections (seriously)
Chinook winds can warm temperatures rapidly, affecting wax

course.primary: Urban point-to-point traversing Anchorage from Service High School to Kincaid Park. The course threads through city greenbelts, boreal forest, and coastal trail with views of Cook Inlet and the Alaska Range. Rolling terrain with moderate climbing.
course.surface: Machine-groomed municipal trails through forest and urban greenbelts
course.grooming: Groomed by Anchorage Nordic Ski Club — excellent standard given terrain variety
features: Cook Inlet coastal trail with views of Denali on clear days
Passage through boreal forest and urban wilderness
Finish at Kincaid Park — a World Cup-caliber Nordic venue
Both classic and freestyle on the same day

nordic_lab_rating.score_note: Alaska's premier citizen ski marathon. The unique point-to-point urban format, coastal scenery, and strong community atmosphere set it apart. Reliable snow and solid organization from the Anchorage Nordic community.
nordic_lab_rating.tier_note: TIER 2 — Alaska's flagship marathon ski race with a one-of-a-kind urban-wilderness format

history.summary: Founded in 1988, the Tour of Anchorage is Alaska's premier Nordic ski marathon. The point-to-point format takes skiers across the city of Anchorage, threading through urban greenbelts, boreal forest, and the Tony Knowles Coastal Trail with views of Cook Inlet and the Alaska Range. Anchorage's deeply rooted Nordic ski culture — with over 250km of groomed trails — provides the infrastructure for one of North America's most unique marathon ski experiences.
notable_facts: One of the few urban point-to-point marathon ski races in the world
Anchorage has over 250km of groomed Nordic trails within city limits
Kincaid Park has hosted World Cup cross-country skiing events
Moose on course are a genuine hazard — officials monitor known crossings
On clear days, Denali (6,190m) is visible from the coastal trail
Anchorage is the only major US city where Nordic skiing is a mainstream sport
